examples.title: RSA Key Generation Algorithm
examples.description: Basic RSA key generation showing computation of public and private keys from primes
examples.code:
BEGIN
 PROCEDURE GenerateRSAKey(keySize):
 // Step 1: Generate two random primes p and q of approximately keySize/2 bits
 REPEAT
 p ← GenerateRandomPrime(keySize/2)
 q ← GenerateRandomPrime(keySize/2)
 UNTIL p ≠ q AND |p - q| > 2^(keySize/2 - 100) // Ensure primes not too close
 
 // Step 2: Compute modulus n
 n ← p × q
 bitLength ← BitLength(n)
 
 // Step 3: Compute Euler's totient φ(n)
 phi ← (p - 1) × (q - 1)
 
 // Step 4: Select public exponent e
 e ← 65537 // Standard choice: 2^16 + 1
 WHILE gcd(e, phi) ≠ 1:
 e ← e + 2
 
 // Step 5: Compute private exponent d
 d ← ModularInverse(e, phi) // Using Extended Euclidean Algorithm
 
 RETURN PublicKey(n, e), PrivateKey(n, d)
 END PROCEDURE
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows RSA key generation. Security depends on: (1) Random prime generation using CSPRNG, (2) Ensuring p and q are not too close (Fermat attack prevention), (3) Using e=65537 ensures d is large (Wiener attack prevention), (4) φ(n) calculation requires prime factors - if n is factored, d can be computed.
examples.timeComplexity: O(k³) for prime generation and modular inverse
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) for storing primes and keys (k = key size in bits)

examples.title: RSA Encryption with OAEP Padding
examples.description: RSA encryption using OAEP (Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding) to prevent chosen ciphertext attacks
examples.code:
BEGIN
 PROCEDURE OAEPEncode(message m, keyLength k):
 // Parameters: hash functions H, MGF; label L
 hLen ← HashLength(SHA-256) // Typically 32 bytes
 
 // Step 1: Initialize and pad
 maxLen ← k - 2hLen - 2
 IF Length(m) > maxLen:
 ERROR "Message too long"
 
 // Step 2: Create encoded message EM
 lHash ← Hash(L) // Usually empty string for encryption
 PS ← 0x00 × (maxLen - |m|) // Padding string
 DB ← lHash || PS || 0x01 || m // Database = lHash || PS || 01 || M
 
 // Step 3: Generate mask for DB
 seed ← RandomBytes(hLen)
 dbMask ← MGF(seed, k - hLen - 1)
 maskedDB ← DB XOR dbMask
 
 // Step 4: Generate mask for seed
 seedMask ← MGF(maskedDB, hLen)
 maskedSeed ← seed XOR seedMask
 
 // Step 5: Create encoded message
 EM ← 0x00 || maskedSeed || maskedDB
 RETURN EM
 
 PROCEDURE RSAEncryptOAEP(message m, PublicKey(n, e)):
 em ← OAEPEncode(m, BitLength(n))
 mInt ← BytesToInteger(em)
 IF mInt >= n:
 ERROR "Message too long for key size"
 c ← mInt^e mod n
 RETURN IntegerToBytes(c)
 END PROCEDURE
END
examples.explanation: OAEP (Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding) provides semantic security against chosen ciphertext attacks. It uses two hash operations (MGF) to generate masks, making encryption probabilistic. The 0x00 prefix ensures the encoded message is smaller than n. Without OAEP (using raw RSA), the cryptosystem is vulnerable to CCA attacks due to multiplicative properties.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) for encoded message and ciphertext

examples.title: Square-and-Multiply with Blinding
examples.description: Constant-time RSA decryption using square-and-multiply with message blinding to prevent timing attacks
examples.code:
BEGIN
 PROCEDURE RSAEncryptBlinded(c, PublicKey(n, e)):
 // Generate random blinding factor
 r ← RandomInteger(1, n-1)
 cBlind ← (c × r^e) mod n // Blind the ciphertext
 
 // Compute blinded result
 mBlind ← FastPower(cBlind, e, n)
 
 // Unblind: multiply by inverse of r
 rInv ← ModularInverse(r, n)
 m ← (mBlind × rInv) mod n
 RETURN m
 
 // Square-and-Multiply with constant-time optimization
 PROCEDURE FastPower(base, exp, mod):
 result ← 1
 base ← base mod mod
 WHILE exp > 0:
 IF (exp AND 1) = 1: // Check least significant bit
 result ← (result × base) mod mod
 base ← (base × base) mod mod
 exp ← exp >> 1 // Right shift (divide by 2)
 RETURN result
 
 PROCEDURE BlindedModularExponentiation(c, d, n):
 // RSA decryption with timing attack protection
 r ← GenerateRandom(n)
 cBlind ← c × r mod n
 mBlind ← FastPower(cBlind, d, n)
 m ← mBlind × r⁻¹ mod n
 RETURN m
 END PROCEDURE
END
examples.explanation: This code shows two timing attack protections: (1) Square-and-multiply uses bit-by-bit exponentiation - without protection, the number of multiplications reveals bits of d, (2) Blinding multiplies the input by a random value before computation and unmasks after, making timing information independent of the actual private key bits. Even if attacker measures timing, they cannot correlate it with d.
examples.timeComplexity: O(k²) for square-and-multiply (k = bit length of exponent)
